title: Incised border pattern tile
subtitle: A 150 mm square tile with carved geometric border
excerpt: A single 150 mm square air-dry clay tile with a geometric border incised at the leather-hard stage and a plain or impressed centre panel. No kiln needed. PVA sealed and pierced for wall hanging.

glossaryTerms.definition: the stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to carve and join
fully air-dried but unfired clay : fragile and absorbent
unfired clay work at any stage from freshly formed to bone-dry

content.text: This tile is 150 mm square and 8 mm thick, made from air-dry clay and decorated with a repeating geometric border carved into the surface at the
leather-hard
stage. The centre panel is left plain or given a single impressed motif. The
greenware
is dried flat, pierced for wall hanging, and sealed with PVA. No kiln is required.
Preparation

Cut 8 mm spacer guides from card. Sketch the border design on paper before touching the clay: a simple repeating unit works best, such as a row of V shapes, a series of short diagonal hatched lines, or a chain of small squares. The incised area inside each border element should be no narrower than 3 mm or the needle tool will struggle to carve it cleanly. Measure the border width on paper and confirm it fits four times around the tile edge with no awkward adjustments at the corners.
Construction
Wedge 250 g of clay and flatten on the wooden board. Place the 8 mm spacer cards on either side and roll the clay to an even 8 mm thickness.
Cut a 150 mm by 150 mm square with the fettling knife against a straight edge. Set the off-cuts aside. Smooth the top surface with a barely damp pottery sponge.

Leave the tile to firm to leather-hard on the board. At this stage the clay holds its shape when lifted but is still damp inside: a fingernail pressed into the surface leaves a mark without sticking. This takes 30 to 90 minutes depending on the clay brand and room humidity.

Mark the border boundary with a soft pencil: draw a line 15 mm in from each edge, forming an inner square of 120 mm by 120 mm. The pencil line will burn off (or in this case, simply fade into the clay surface) and will not show in the finished piece.

Using the needle tool, incise the outer and inner edges of the border strip. Draw each line in a single continuous pass. Keep the needle nearly vertical and pull toward you with steady, even pressure : do not press so hard that the clay tears, and do not press so lightly that the line is too shallow to read.

Divide each side of the border into equal segments with light pencil marks. Using your paper sketch as reference, incise the repeating geometric elements within the border strip: diagonal hatch lines, V shapes, chevrons, or a series of small triangles. Use the sgraffito tool for wider, flatter grooves and the needle tool for finer lines.

For the centre panel: leave it plain, or press a single stamp or found object (a button, a small pebble, a coin) firmly into the centre to leave an impressed motif. Press straight down and lift straight up.
Pierce a 5 mm hanging hole at the top centre, 15 mm down from the upper edge, using the needle tool. Rotate slowly to widen the hole without cracking.
Drying
Leave flat on the wooden board until
bone-dry

: typically 24 to 2 days. Square tiles with incised borders are prone to slight corner warping as the thinner border strips dry faster than the centre. If corners begin to lift, place a small weight at each corner as soon as you notice. Do not try to flatten the tile after bone-dry : at that stage any forced bending will crack the incised lines.
Finishing

Once bone-dry, seal all surfaces : front, back, and all four edges : with undiluted PVA. Work the brush into the incised grooves so no unseal clay is left exposed inside the carved lines. Apply two coats, leaving 30 minutes between them. Thread picture wire or cord through the hanging hole for display. Alternatively, adhere a picture hanger hook to the reverse with a small amount of PVA and allow to cure overnight.
Care

Display indoors, away from sustained moisture. Dust with a dry cloth. The incised grooves trap dust: blow out with a puff of air or use a soft paint brush. If a carved line chips at the corner of the tile, smooth the edge with fine sandpaper and touch up with undiluted PVA.
Variations

Colour accent: after sealing, rub acrylic paint diluted with water into the incised grooves. Wipe the raised surface clean with a damp cloth while the paint is still wet. The colour settles into the carving only.
Double border: incise a second, narrower border inside the first for a more structured frame effect. Leave 5 mm between the two border strips.
Set of three: make three tiles with the same border but different centre motifs : a leaf, a simple floral, and a geometric : and hang them in a row.
Incise a name or a short word in block letters across the centre panel before bone-dry. The letter height works best at 20 to 30 mm for readability.

rows.problem: Incised lines are ragged with torn clay edges
rows.cause: Clay too soft when carving : still too wet to hold a clean cut
rows.fix: Wait until the clay is properly leather-hard before incising. Test: the surface should yield under a fingernail without sticking.

rows.problem: Incised lines are too shallow and disappear when dry
rows.cause: Insufficient pressure or clay too stiff
rows.fix: Increase the depth of the groove: at leather-hard the line should be clearly visible when you hold the tile at a low angle to the light. If the clay has dried past leather-hard, dampen the surface lightly and wait 10 minutes before continuing.

rows.problem: Border pattern does not align at corners
rows.cause: Geometric repeat not measured before carving
rows.fix: Always mark the border into equal segments on all four sides before cutting the first element. Adjust the repeat unit spacing slightly at corners to close any gap.

rows.cause: Hole pierced when clay too dry, or rotated too forcefully
rows.fix: Pierce at leather-hard with a slow, rotating motion. If the hole is already cracked, reinforce by applying a thin ring of clay slip around the hole at leather-hard, allow to dry, and re-drill once bone-dry with a narrow nail.

rows.problem: PVA pools in the carving grooves and looks milky
rows.cause: PVA too thick or too much applied in one coat
rows.fix: Thin PVA slightly with a few drops of water and work into the grooves with a liner brush, then wick out excess by drawing the brush along the groove edge. Let each coat dry fully clear before adding the next.
